questions.question: More than half of fetuses with Down Syndrome (Trisomy 21) have which congenital heart issues?
questions.detail: AV canal and VSD are the most common cardiac defects in Down Syndrome.

questions.question: What is the most common intra-abdominal pathology associated with Down Syndrome?
questions.detail: Duodenal atresia is the primary intra-abdominal association.

questions.question: At what gestational age is it typically 'hard to see' duodenal atresia on ultrasound?
choices: It is visible from the 1st trimester
questions.detail: This pathology is generally difficult to visualize prior to 22 weeks.

questions.question: Nuchal Lucency (Translucency) is typically measured between which weeks?
questions.detail: The measurement window for nuchal lucency is 9 to 12 weeks.

questions.question: A Nuchal Lucency measurement is considered abnormal if it is greater than:
questions.detail: A measurement > 3 mm is linked to chromosomal abnormalities.

questions.question: Nuchal Fold thickness is measured during the second trimester; what is the abnormal threshold?
questions.detail: Fold thickness > 6 mm in the second trimester is non-specific but concerning.

questions.question: An 'Echogenic Focus in the Cardiac Ventricle' (calcified papillary muscle) increases the risk of Down Syndrome by how much?
questions.detail: This finding is associated with a 4x increased risk for Down Syndrome.

questions.question: Choroid plexus cysts on a fetal survey are not specific but are seen more frequently in:
questions.detail: Choroid plexus cysts are a more frequent finding in Trisomy 18.

questions.question: In the first trimester Down Syndrome screening, maternal blood is analyzed for free Beta hCG and:
questions.detail: Maternal serum screening includes free Beta hCG and PAPP-A.

questions.question: Amniotic Band Syndrome is caused by disruption of the amnion before:
questions.detail: Disruption before 10 weeks allows fetal parts to get tangled in the chorionic cavity.

questions.question: Which of the following is a potential severe result of Amniotic Band Syndrome?
questions.detail: Bands can cause devastating injuries like decapitation or limb amputation.

questions.question: Ultrasound diagnosis of Fetal Hydrops requires the presence of at least how many findings (e.g., pleural effusion, ascites)?
questions.detail: Hydrops requires at least two findings such as pleural/pericardial effusion, ascites, or edema.

questions.question: A 'thickened placenta' (> 4–5 cm) is an indirect sign of hydrops nicknamed:
questions.detail: Placentomegaly is a term used for an abnormally thick placenta in hydrops.

questions.question: Which Doppler study is useful for predicting hydrops by showing an increase in peak systolic flow?
questions.detail: MCA Doppler showing increased peak systolic flow can predict the onset of hydrops.

questions.question: Fetal Tachycardia is defined as a heart rate greater than:
questions.detail: A fetal heart rate > 180 bpm defines tachycardia.

questions.question: Fetal Bradycardia is defined as a heart rate less than:
questions.detail: A fetal heart rate < 100 bpm defines bradycardia.

questions.question: The 'Double Bubble' sign on antenatal imaging represents:
questions.detail: The 'double bubble' sign is diagnostic of duodenal atresia.

questions.question: Fetal bowel is considered 'echogenic' (too bright) if its brightness is equal to the:
questions.detail: Bowel echogenicity is graded against the brightness of the fetal iliac crest.

questions.question: Which condition is a 'reflex' diagnosis when massively enlarged bilateral fetal kidneys and oligohydramnios are seen?
questions.detail: Massively enlarged bilateral kidneys and oligohydramnios is the fetal hallmark of ARPKD.

questions.question: Posterior Urethral Valves (PUV) classically present on fetal imaging as:
choices: Bilateral hydro and a thick-walled bladder
questions.detail: PUV results in high-pressure obstruction causing bilateral hydronephrosis and a thick bladder.

questions.question: A short femur is defined as a measurement below which percentile?
questions.detail: A femur length < 5th percentile is used to define a short femur.

questions.question: Which is the most common tumor of the fetus or infant?
questions.detail: Sacrococcygeal teratoma is the most frequent tumor encountered in infancy.

questions.question: 'Vasa Previa Type 1' occurs when fetal vessels connect to a:
questions.detail: Type 1 vasa previa involves vessels connecting to a velamentous cord insertion.

questions.question: 'Vasa Previa Type 2' is associated with vessels connecting to which placental variant?
questions.detail: Type 2 vasa previa involves vessels connecting to an accessory or bilobed placenta.

questions.question: A single vertical fluid pocket of less than 2 cm is a component of the:
questions.detail: The BPP requires at least one vertical fluid pocket > 2 cm for points.

questions.question: In fetal circulation, the umbilical vein carries blood from the placenta to the:
questions.detail: Umbilical vein blood reaches the IVC/liver via the ductus venosus.

questions.question: The fetal 'Cystic Rhombencephalon' is a normal structure seen in the posterior fossa at:
questions.detail: This is a normal posterior fossa landmark at 6-8 weeks and should not be mistaken for pathology.

questions.question: 'Physiologic Midgut Herniation' into the umbilical cord is a normal process occurring at:
questions.detail: Normal physiologic herniation is seen between weeks 9 and 11.

questions.question: The most common primary lung malignancy in children is:
questions.detail: PPB is the most frequent primary pediatric lung cancer.

questions.question: Which 'Michaelis-Gutmann' body containing condition is associated with chronic E. coli UTIs?
questions.detail: Malakoplakia features Michaelis-Gutmann bodies and is related to chronic infections.

questions.question: The 'Lemon Sign' on a fetal head scan typically disappears after:
questions.detail: The lemon sign (frontal indentation) usually resolves after 24 weeks.

questions.question: What is the classic look of CMV infection in the fetal brain?
choices: High T2 signal in white matter
questions.detail: CMV characteristically causes periventricular calcifications.

questions.question: Which TORCH infection has the HIGHEST association with polymicrogyria?
questions.detail: Among TORCH infections, CMV is most strongly linked to polymicrogyria.

questions.question: Toxoplasmosis infection in the fetus classically targets the:
questions.detail: Fetal toxoplasmosis typically targets the basal ganglia.

questions.question: HSV-2 infection in the newborn prefers endothelial cells, often resulting in bad encephalomalacia and:
questions.detail: HSV-2 can lead to severe encephalomalacia and hydranencephaly.

questions.question: A cervix is considered 'short' (incompetent) if its canal measures less than:
questions.detail: A measurement < 2.5 cm is the threshold for a short cervix.

questions.question: Maternal hydronephrosis occurs in what percentage of pregnancies?
questions.detail: Mechanical compression causes hydronephrosis in 80% of pregnant women.

questions.question: Maternal hydronephrosis tends to affect which side more often?
choices: Right side in the 1st trimester only
questions.detail: The right side is affected 5x more often due to uterine dextrorotation.

questions.question: Fibroids tend to grow in early pregnancy secondary to elevated:
questions.detail: High estrogen in early pregnancy promotes fibroid growth.

questions.question: In later pregnancy, which hormone can have an inhibitory effect on fibroid growth?
questions.detail: Progesterone levels later in pregnancy can inhibit further fibroid growth.

questions.question: Uterine rupture is most common in the 3rd trimester and occurs most frequently at the site of:
questions.detail: Rupture occurs most often through a prior C-section scar.

questions.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for uterine rupture?
questions.detail: Unicornuate uterus and prior uterine surgery are significant risk factors.

questions.question: HELLP Syndrome is defined by Hemolysis, Low Platelets, and:
questions.detail: HELLP stands for Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, and Low Platelets.

questions.question: What is the characteristic imaging finding of HELLP Syndrome?
questions.detail: The hallmark imaging finding for HELLP is a subcapsular hepatic hematoma.

questions.question: Peripartum Cardiomyopathy is a dilated cardiomyopathy that can be seen from the last month of pregnancy up to how many months postpartum?
questions.detail: The clinical window for this cardiomyopathy is from late pregnancy to 5 months postpartum.

questions.question: Sheehan Syndrome is defined as pituitary apoplexy resulting from:
questions.detail: Large-volume postpartum hemorrhage leads to hypotension and pituitary apoplexy.

questions.question: In the ACUTE phase of Sheehan Syndrome, the pituitary will likely appear:
questions.detail: Acute hemorrhage/apoplexy in the pituitary typically appears T1 bright.

questions.question: The 'late look' of Sheehan Syndrome on MRI is:
choices: Ring enhancement around an empty sella
questions.detail: Late-stage Sheehan results in an empty sella with a ring of enhancement.

questions.question: Ovarian Vein Thrombophlebitis (OVT) is a cause of postpartum fever that affects the RIGHT side how much more often than the left?
choices: It is only on the left
questions.detail: OVT occurs on the right side 5 times more frequently than the left.

questions.question: Retained Products of Conception (RPOC) typically present with:
choices: Continued bleeding after delivery or abortion
questions.detail: Ongoing postpartum bleeding is the most common clinical presentation for RPOC.

questions.question: RPOC should be suspected if the endometrial thickness is greater than which threshold following dilation and curettage (D&C)?
questions.detail: A thickness > 5 mm post-D&C is concerning for RPOC.

questions.question: General irregular endometrial thickening of greater than how many mm is a sneaky way to show RPOC?
questions.detail: Irregular thickening > 10 mm often represents retained products.

questions.question: Endometritis characteristically features which triad of symptoms?
choices: Fever, uterine tenderness, and recent C-section
questions.detail: Postpartum fever and tenderness are the classic clinical markers.

questions.question: Which of the following grow specifically during pregnancy?
choices: Babies, Splenic Artery Aneurysms, Renal AMLs, and Fibroids
questions.detail: For multiple-choice purposes, these four things are known to grow during pregnancy.

questions.question: A subcapsular hepatic hematoma in a pregnant woman with low platelets is pathognomonic for:
questions.detail: This combination is the classic exam presentation for HELLP.

questions.question: A 'trapped uterus' refers to persistent:
questions.detail: A uterus 'trapped' in retroflexion by adhesions is a risk for rupture.

questions.question: Macrosomia refers to babies that are 'too big,' defined as being above which percentile?
questions.detail: The 90th percentile is the standard definition for fetal macrosomia.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of fetal macrosomia?
questions.detail: Maternal diabetes is the leading contributor to macrosomia.

questions.question: Erb's Palsy is a brachial plexus injury (C5-C6) most commonly associated with:
questions.detail: Shoulder dystocia in large babies is the classic mechanism for Erb's Palsy.

questions.question: Oligohydramnios is defined as an Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) less than:
questions.detail: An AFI < 5 cm defines oligohydramnios.

questions.question: Polyhydramnios is defined as an AFI greater than:
questions.detail: An AFI > 20 cm or a single pocket > 8 cm defines polyhydramnios.

questions.question: If you see too little fluid (oligohydramnios), the reflex thought should be:
choices: The baby's kidneys aren't working (not peeing)
questions.detail: Low fluid often means the baby is not producing urine due to renal issues.

questions.question: If you see too much fluid (polyhydramnios), the reflex thought should be:
choices: The baby's kidneys are too good
questions.detail: Excess fluid suggests the baby cannot swallow it, often due to GI obstructions.

questions.question: A 'Two Vessel Cord' (missing one artery) is seen in 1% of pregnancies and is associated with:
questions.detail: A single umbilical artery increases the risk for anomalies and maternal diabetes.

questions.question: Which maternal artery should NOT have its systolic/diastolic ratio exceed 3 at 34 weeks?
questions.detail: A ratio > 3 in the umbilical artery suggests pre-eclampsia and IUGR.

questions.question: A 'Painless Vaginal Bleeding' in the 3rd trimester is the clinical trigger for:
questions.detail: The hallmark of placenta previa is painless bleeding near term.

questions.question: A 'PAINFUL' premature separation of the placenta is:
questions.detail: Unlike previa, abruption is characteristically painful.

questions.question: 'Placenta Creta' risk factors include advanced maternal age, placenta previa, and:
questions.detail: Prior C-sections and placenta previa are the primary risk factors for Creta.

questions.question: Placenta Chorioangioma is a hamartoma that can cause high output failure (hydrops) if it is larger than:
questions.detail: Chorioangiomas > 4 cm can lead to fetal high-output heart failure.

questions.question: 'Vasa Previa' vessels that cross the internal cervical os are at high risk of:
questions.detail: These fetal vessels can rupture during labor, causing catastrophic bleeding.

questions.question: Dizygotic (not-identical) twins are always:
questions.detail: Fraternal twins always have separate chorions and sacs.

questions.question: Monozygotic (identical) twins that split before 8 days are:
questions.detail: Splitting before day 8 results in two amniotic sacs.

questions.question: Splitting of a fertilized ovum AFTER 8 days results in:
questions.detail: A split after 8 days means the twins share one amniotic sac.

questions.question: Conjoined twins result from a very late split, typically occurring after:
questions.detail: Splitting at 13 days or later leads to incomplete separation (conjoined).

questions.question: The 'Twin-Peak Sign' (beak-like tongue of membrane) EXCLUDES which type of pregnancy?
questions.detail: The 'beak' proves it is dichorionic, which excludes sharing a chorion.

questions.question: The 'T Sign' (absence of a twin peak) is the hallmark for:
questions.detail: A 'T' intersection (thin membrane meeting placenta) defines monochorionicity.

questions.question: Membrane thickness is used to differentiate twins; a 'thick' membrane (1–2 mm) suggests:
questions.detail: Dichorionic membranes have 4 layers and appear thicker (1-2 mm).

questions.question: Twin-Twin Transfusion Syndrome (TTTS) occurs ONLY in which type of twins?
questions.detail: TTTS requires a single placenta with vascular communications.

questions.question: In TTTS, which twin is actually the one that typically gets hydrops and dies?
questions.detail: The recipient twin receives too much blood, leading to fluid overload/hydrops.

questions.question: TRAP sequence and TTTS are both characterized by:
questions.detail: Both involve dramatic fluid shifts with one twin having too much and the other too little.

questions.question: 'Twin-Embolization Syndrome' refers to the transfer of necrotic tissue from a dead baby to a living one; this can ONLY occur in:
questions.detail: Shared circulation is required for necrotic tissue to transfer to the survivor.

questions.question: What are the potential results of Twin-Embolization Syndrome for the surviving fetus?
questions.detail: Survivor risks include DIC, ischemia, and infarction.

questions.question: TAPS (Twin Anemia-Polycythemia Sequence) is characterized by hemoglobin differences but does NOT feature:
questions.detail: TAPS is a slow flow hemoglobin shift without the fluid sequence seen in TTTS.

questions.question: Monoamniotic twins (splitting after 8 days) have worse outcomes because they:
questions.detail: Cord entanglement is a major cause of death in monoamniotics.

questions.question: As a general rule, the LATER the fertilized ovum splits:
choices: The more likely they are dizygotic
questions.detail: Risk increases significantly as the split happens later in development.

questions.question: Dichorionic diamniotic fetuses can have either separate placentas or a:
questions.detail: Separate placentas can fuse together and appear as one mass on ultrasound.

questions.question: Which twin 'battle tactic' involves looking for the 'beak-like tongue' between membranes?
questions.detail: The 'beak' confirms a dichorionic diamniotic status.

questions.question: 'Azygos continuation' of the IVC is a classic association for which heterotaxia?
questions.detail: Azygos continuation is pathognomonic for polysplenia.

questions.question: 'Reversed Aorta/IVC' is a feature of which heterotaxia syndrome?
questions.detail: The flipped aorta and IVC relationship defines asplenia.

questions.question: How many lung fissures are found in a 'Bilateral Right Sided' (Asplenia) patient?
choices: Three on the right, zero on the left
questions.detail: Asplenia means both sides look like the right lung, which has two fissures.

questions.question: How many lung fissures are found in a 'Bilateral Left Sided' (Polysplenia) patient?
questions.detail: Polysplenia means both sides look like the left lung, which has one fissure.

questions.question: Biliary atresia is associated with which condition in 10% of cases?
questions.detail: 10% of polysplenia cases have associated biliary atresia.

questions.question: 'Situs Solitus' is a fancy way of saying:
questions.detail: Solitus means standard normal organ arrangement.

questions.question: 'Situs Inversus Totalis' refers to:
questions.detail: Total mirror image arrangement of all thoracic and abdominal organs.

questions.question: 'Situs Ambiguus' is another term for:
questions.detail: Ambiguus refers to the disorganized anatomy of heterotaxy.

questions.question: A vertical fluid pocket > 8 cm on ultrasound is the definition of:
questions.detail: A single pocket exceeding 8 cm is polyhydramnios.

questions.question: 'Amniotic Band Syndrome' can cause which of the following severe complications?
questions.detail: Constricting bands can literally sever fetal limbs or heads.

questions.question: Monozygotic twins: splitting at 13 days or later leads to:
questions.detail: Very late splitting (after day 13) results in incomplete division.

questions.question: Which twin sign 'excludes a monochorionic pregnancy'?
questions.detail: The twin-peak sign confirms separate chorions.

questions.question: In TTTS, the fat twin usually does worse and the 'skinny kid' outcome is:
questions.detail: The donor twin often has a better clinical prognosis than the recipient.
